summary: The review of the Student Research Agreement indicates a moderate risk posture, with several clauses requiring further attention. Key issues include Intellectual Property (IP) provisions, which must be referred to Auckland UniServices Ltd for compliance, and ambiguous or incomplete clauses related to confidentiality, liability, and publication rights that deviate from UoA's preferred positions. Immediate next steps involve escalating flagged clauses to the appropriate UoA teams, including UniServices for IP matters, the Senior Manager for Research Grants and Contracts for confidentiality and publication concerns, and the Tax and Compliance team for liability inconsistencies.

flags.clause_title: 2.2 Obligations
flags.snippet: 2.2 Obligations Student’s termination;
flags.rationale: The clause text is incomplete and does not provide sufficient detail to confirm alignment with UoA's Preferred or Acceptable positions on Termination. Specifically, it does not address mutual two-way termination for material breach or insolvency, nor does it clarify termination costs or accrued rights. The lack of clarity triggers escalation to the Senior Manager, Research Grants and Contracts for review and potential amendment.

flags.rationale: The clause is poorly drafted and ambiguous, making it difficult to determine compliance with UoA's Preferred or Acceptable positions on Publication. It does not clearly preserve the Principal Investigator's or Student's right to publish, nor does it specify reasonable stand-down periods or embargo limits. This materially deviates from UoA Position #POS-12, which mandates clear rights for publication and reasonable restrictions. Escalation to the Senior Manager, Research Grants and Contracts is required to clarify and redraft the clause.

flags.clause_title: 4.5 The Funding is exclusive of GST, and the University
flags.snippet: 4.5 The Funding is exclusive of GST, and the University is responsible for all taxation liabilities, rates, and levies payable in relation to the Funding.
flags.rationale: The clause materially deviates from UoA Position POS-06 on Taxation. While the clause correctly states that funding is exclusive of GST, it improperly assigns responsibility for all taxation liabilities, rates, and levies to the University. UoA's preferred position is that overseas contracts should state costs as 'excluding any applicable taxes and duties, payable by the Client on any taxable supplies.' This deviation requires escalation to the Tax and Compliance team for review.

flags.snippet: 8.2 Each Party will only use the name of another Party or its Affiliates in any publication, advertising, or news release relating to or referring to the Project with the prior written approval of that Party. This appro…
flags.rationale: This clause primarily concerns the use of names and branding, which aligns with UoA Position #POS-14 on Publicity. The preferred position is that neither party can use the names or brands of the other or their personnel without prior written consent. However, the clause introduces an exception allowing the Client to include the University’s and Student’s names in a list of current research projects or collaborations in its annual report. This exception is not covered under the UoA Preferred or Acceptable tiers and could lead to reputational risks. Escalation to University Marketing (brand@auckland.ac.nz) is required to assess whether this exception is acceptable.

flags.snippet: 9.2 Either Party may terminate this Agreement with thirty (30) days’ notice to the other Party in the following circumstances:
flags.rationale: The clause introduces termination rights that are not aligned with UoA's Preferred Position for mutual two-way termination for material breach or insolvency, nor does it align with the Acceptable tier for termination for convenience (which is restricted to Consulting contracts with specific conditions). The clause lacks specificity regarding termination costs, accrued rights, and uncancellable obligations, which are critical elements of UoA's Preferred Position. This deviation requires escalation to the Senior Manager Research Grants and Contracts for review and potential negotiation.

flags.clause_title: 11.3 To the extent permitted by law, the liability of each Party
flags.snippet: 11.3 To the extent permitted by law, the liability of each Party to the other Party in relation to any and all claims in any manner related to this Agreement (whether in contract, tort, including negligence, strict liability, by statute or otherwise) for all events that occur in connection with this Agreement, will be limited in total to the greater of (i) all amounts paid or payable by [redacted] under this Agreement, or [redacted].
flags.rationale: The clause materially deviates from UoA Position #POS-07 on Liability Limitations and Exclusions. The liability cap is stated as 'the greater of' amounts paid or payable, which is inconsistent with the Preferred Position that liability must be capped at the lesser of contract value or NZD$500,000. Additionally, the clause does not explicitly exclude consequential or indirect losses, nor does it address exclusions for loss of data, profit, revenue, savings, business, or goodwill. This deviation requires escalation to the Research Grants and Contracts Delegations for review and potential amendment.

flags.snippet: 11.4.2 loss of profits, business, opportunity, or anticipated savings (whether directly or indirectly arising). To avoid doubt, loss that arises in the ordinary course as a natural result of a breach will not be conside…
flags.rationale: The clause materially deviates from UoA Position #POS-07 on Liability Limitations and Exclusions. While it excludes loss of profits, business, opportunity, or anticipated savings, it introduces ambiguity by stating that losses arising in the ordinary course as a natural result of a breach will not be considered consequential, indirect, incidental, or special. This contradicts the Preferred Position, which explicitly excludes all consequential or indirect losses without such qualifications. The ambiguity could expose UoA to liability for losses that are not clearly excluded. Escalation to Research Grants and Contracts Delegations is required.

flags.clause_title: 4.4 If a dispute arises in connection with an invoice,
flags.snippet: 4.4 If a dispute arises in connection with an invoice, [redacted] may withhold the disputed amount (but not any amount which is not in dispute) until the dispute is resolved. [redacted] (acting reasonably) will advise the University of any disputed amount in an invoice prior to the due date for payment and the University will have the opportunity to provide further information to support its claim for payment of such disputed amount within five (5) Working Days for consideration. If the amount claimed is then still in dispute, either Party may refer the matter to dispute resolution in accordance with clause 10.
flags.rationale: This clause addresses payment terms and disputes over invoices, which are sensitive under UoA Position #POS-15. While the clause allows withholding of disputed amounts and provides a mechanism for resolution, it deviates from the preferred position of payment terms being '20th of the month following date of invoice.' The clause does not explicitly align with the preferred or acceptable tier, and the dispute resolution mechanism introduces additional complexity. Escalation to the Credit and Collections Manager at STC is recommended for confirmation.

flags.clause_title: 10.1 Subject to any requirement for urgent interlocutory
flags.snippet: 10.1 Subject to any requirement for urgent interlocutory relief, before resorting to external dispute resolution mechanisms (including litigation), the Parties must attempt to settle by negotiation any dispute in relati…
flags.rationale: The clause addresses Dispute Resolution, which is a UoA-sensitive topic under Position #POS-18. While it aligns with the preferred position in requiring negotiation before external mechanisms, it does not explicitly include escalation to senior management or mediation before arbitration/litigation, nor does it specify that all proceedings must be in English. The clause does preserve the ability to seek urgent interlocutory relief, which is consistent with the preferred position. However, the absence of explicit mediation and escalation steps makes it fall short of full alignment with the preferred tier, placing it in the acceptable tier. No escalation is required unless the clause deviates further.
flags.text: 10.1 Subject to any requirement for urgent interlocutory relief, before resorting to external dispute resolution mechanisms (including litigation), the Parties must attempt to settle by negotiation any dispute in relation to this Agreement, including by referring the dispute to any person who may be able to assist in some form of resolution of the dispute. Such an attempt will be required to be made within ten (10) days of either Party giving notice to the other Party that there is a dispute and that it is wished to resolve that dispute by negotiation.

flags.clause_title: 10.2 If a dispute is not settled by the Parties pursuant to
flags.snippet: 10.2 If a dispute is not settled by the Parties pursuant to clause 10.1 within twenty (20) days of either Party first sending to the others a notice that a dispute exists, the dispute will be submitted by agreement betw…
flags.rationale: The clause addresses Dispute Resolution, which is a UoA-sensitive topic. While it includes mediation and expert determination, it does not explicitly align with the Preferred Position requiring escalation to senior management followed by mediation before arbitration/litigation, nor does it mention urgent interlocutory action through courts. However, the clause does not materially deviate from the Acceptable tier, as dispute clauses for NZ and International Funding Agency contracts cannot always be altered. Escalation to the Senior Manager Research Grants and Contracts or General Counsel is recommended for confirmation.
flags.text: 10.2 If a dispute is not settled by the Parties pursuant to clause 10.1 within twenty (20) days of either Party first sending to the others a notice that a dispute exists, the dispute will be submitted by agreement between the Parties for mediation or expert determination by an appropriate person agreed by the Parties (or if the Parties cannot so agree within twenty-five (25) Working Days of the dispute notice being sent, by the President of the Law Society or their nominee), provided always that neither Party will be obliged to accept as final any expert determination by any expert nominated by the Parties.

flags.clause_title: 11.5 Nothing in the Agreement excludes or limits either
flags.snippet: 11.5 Nothing in the Agreement excludes or limits either Party’s liability to the other which cannot lawfully be excluded or limited. Each Party’s liability in respect of a claim will be reduced proportionately to the extent that the other Party’s breach, fraud or negligent act or omission caused or contributed to the claim. Each Party agrees that it will use commercially reasonable efforts to minimize any damages it may incur as a result of the other Party’s failure to perform pursuant to this Agreement.
flags.rationale: This clause addresses liability limitations and exclusions, which are sensitive under UoA Position #POS-07. While it aligns with the principle that liability cannot be excluded where prohibited by law, it does not explicitly address the monetary cap or exclusion of indirect/consequential losses as required by the Preferred tier. The proportional reduction for contributory negligence and the obligation to mitigate damages are acceptable elements, but the clause lacks clarity on total liability limits and exclusions for indirect losses, which are critical under UoA policy. Contract Manager should confirm alignment with Research Contract Delegations and escalate if necessary.

flags.snippet: 10. Disputes
flags.rationale: The clause aligns with UoA Preferred Contracting Position POS-18 on Dispute Resolution. It requires parties to discuss disputes in good faith before arbitration or litigation, includes escalation to senior management and mediation, and allows for urgent interlocutory action through courts. This matches the preferred tier of POS-18.

flags.snippet: 10.3 If the Parties cannot resolve any dispute in accordance with clauses 10.1 and 10.2 then and only then may the Parties proceed to litigation in respect of such dispute, provided that nothing in this clause 10 will prevent either Party from commencing court proceedings for the purposes of seeking urgent interlocutory relief.

flags.clause_title: 11. Liability
flags.snippet: 11. Liability
flags.rationale: The clause aligns with UoA Position #POS-07 on Liability Limitations and Exclusions. The liability cap is set at NZD$30,000, which is well below the NZD$500,000 threshold. Liability is limited to direct losses, and consequential or indirect losses (including loss of profit, revenue, savings, business, and goodwill) are explicitly excluded. This matches the Preferred tier of UoA's contracting positions.
flags.text: 11. Liability

flags.clause_title: 11.1 The Parties acknowledge that this is an Agreement to
flags.snippet: 11.1 The Parties acknowledge that this is an Agreement to carry out research, the Results of which cannot be predicted. Further, the Parties acknowledge that the Project will be undertaken by the Student who may not be experienced in research of any kind and whose work cannot be controlled by the University, the University Supervisor, or the Supervisor. The University gives no warranty or undertaking as to the timing of any part of the Project or the outcome of the Project and makes no warranty as to the suitability of the Project, research, or Results for any purpose.
flags.rationale: This clause aligns with UoA Position #POS-10 on Warranties. It explicitly excludes all warranties regarding the timing, outcome, or suitability of the research and results, which matches the Preferred tier. The clause also acknowledges the unpredictable nature of research and the limited control over the Student's work, which is consistent with the University's standard approach to disclaiming warranties in research agreements.
